  • Ou tapez votre quartier (comme « centre-ville de Kingston »)

docs.title: Recherches de crise
docs.text:
Si vous recherchez des mots de crise comme « suicide » ou « abus », vous verrez :

  1. Une bannière rouge avec des numéros d'urgence
  2. Services de crise en haut des résultats
  3. Options d'aide immédiate (téléphone, texto, chat)

Si vous êtes en danger immédiat, appelez le 911.

docs.title: Cartes de service
docs.text: Chaque résultat de recherche affiche une carte de service avec :

docs.title: 1. Nom du service
docs.text: Le nom de l'organisation ou du programme.

docs.title: 2. Description
docs.text: Ce que fait le service et qui il aide.

docs.title: 3. Coordonnées
docs.text:
• 📞 Téléphone : Appuyez pour appeler (sur mobile)
  • 📧 Courriel : Appuyez pour envoyer un courriel
  • 🌐 Site Web : Cliquez pour visiter leur site
  • 📍 Adresse : Obtenir des directions

docs.title: 4. Heures d'ouverture
docs.text:
Quand le service est ouvert. Exemples :

  • « Lundi-Vendredi, 9h-17h »
  • « 24/7 » (toujours ouvert)
  • « Appelez pour les heures »

docs.title: 5. Comment accéder (Script d'accès)
docs.text:
Instructions étape par étape :

  • Pour qui est-ce ? (éligibilité)
  • Quoi apporter (pièce d'identité, documents)
  • Comment obtenir de l'aide (sans rendez-vous, appel, référence nécessaire)

docs.title: 6. Badge de vérification
docs.text:
Montre comment nous avons vérifié ce service :

  • N1 (Basique) : Nous avons confirmé qu'il existe
  • N2 (Vérifié) : Nous les avons contactés directement
  • N3 (Certifié) : Partenariat officiel (meilleur)

Des niveaux plus élevés signifient des informations plus fiables.

docs.title: 7. Langues
docs.text: Langues offertes par le service (anglais, français, etc.).

docs.title: 8. Accessibilité
docs.text: Informations sur l'accès en fauteuil roulant, le stationnement, le transport en commun.

docs.title: Que faire s'il n'y a pas de résultats ?
docs.text:
Si vous voyez « Aucun résultat trouvé », essayez :

  1. Utilisez des mots différents
  2. « nourriture » au lieu de « épicerie »

  3. « logement » au lieu de « refuge »

  4. Vérifiez votre orthographe

  5. Parcourez les catégories sous la zone de recherche

  6. Retirez les filtres (comme « Ouvert maintenant »)

  7. Recherchez un sujet plus large

  8. « santé » au lieu de « clinique du diabète »

docs.title: Voir les services sur une carte
docs.text:
Étape 1 : Cliquez sur le bouton « Vue carte » (en haut à droite des résultats).

Étape 2 : Voyez les épingles montrant où se trouve chaque service.

Étape 3 : Cliquez sur une épingle pour voir les détails de ce service.

Étape 4 : Cliquez sur « Charger l’aperçu de la carte » si vous voulez voir la carte intégrée.

Étape 5 : Cliquez sur « Obtenir des directions » pour ouvrir Google Maps directement.

Remarque : L’aperçu de la carte est désactivé par défaut pour protéger la vie privée. Il ne se charge que si vous choisissez de l’afficher.

docs.title: Autoriser l'accès à la localisation
docs.text:
Pour voir les services près de chez vous :

Étape 1 : Cliquez sur « Utiliser ma localisation » ou autorisez la localisation lorsque demandé.

Étape 2 : Votre navigateur demandera la permission. Cliquez sur « Autoriser ».

Étape 3 : Les résultats seront maintenant triés par distance de vous.

Remarque : Votre localisation n'est jamais sauvegardée ou partagée. Elle ne fonctionne que sur votre appareil.

docs.title: Fonctionnalités d'accessibilité
docs.text: Nous avons construit cette plateforme pour qu'elle fonctionne pour tout le monde.

docs.title: Navigation au clavier
docs.text:
Vous pouvez utiliser ce site sans souris :

  • Tab : Passer au bouton ou lien suivant
  • Maj + Tab : Revenir en arrière
  • Entrée : Cliquer sur un bouton ou un lien
  • Barre d'espace : Cocher une case
  • Échap : Fermer une fenêtre contextuelle

docs.title: Lecteurs d'écran
docs.text:
Ce site fonctionne avec les lecteurs d'écran comme :

  • JAWS
  • NVDA
  • VoiceOver (Mac/iPhone)
  • TalkBack (Android)

Conseils pour les lecteurs d'écran :

  • Les résultats sont annoncés pendant que vous recherchez
  • Utilisez les en-têtes pour passer entre les sections
  • Les cartes de service ont des étiquettes claires

docs.title: Taille du texte
docs.text:
Agrandissez ou réduisez le texte :

  • Dans votre navigateur : Appuyez sur Ctrl + (Windows) ou Cmd + (Mac)
  • Sur mobile : Utilisez les paramètres d'accessibilité de votre appareil

docs.title: Contraste élevé
docs.text:
Si vous avez une basse vision :

  1. Activez Mode contraste élevé dans votre système d'exploitation
  2. Le site ajustera automatiquement les couleurs

docs.title: Mouvement réduit
docs.text:
Si les animations vous donnent le vertige :

  1. Activez Réduire le mouvement dans les paramètres de votre appareil
  2. Le site affichera moins d'animation

docs.title: Changer de langue
docs.text:
Étape 1 : Regardez dans le coin supérieur droit de la page.

Étape 2 : Cliquez sur le menu déroulant de langue (affiche votre langue actuelle).

Étape 3 : Sélectionnez votre langue :

  • English (Anglais)
  • Français
  • 中文 (Chinois)
  • العربية (Arabe)
  • Português (Portugais)
  • Español (Espagnol)
  • ਪੰਜਾਬੀ (Pendjabi)

Étape 4 : Le site entier passera à cette langue.

docs.title: Langues d'information de service
docs.text: Certains services offrent de l'aide dans plusieurs langues. Vous verrez cela dans la carte de service sous « Langues offertes ».

docs.title: Qu'est-ce que le mode hors ligne ?
docs.text:
Le site fonctionne même sans Internet. Cela aide si vous :

  • Avez une connexion lente
  • Êtes dans un endroit sans WiFi
  • Voulez économiser les données mobiles

docs.title: Comment ça marche
docs.text:
Première visite (avec Internet) :

  1. Visitez le site
  2. Le site enregistre les informations de service sur votre appareil
  3. Vous voyez un message : « Prêt à fonctionner hors ligne »

Visites suivantes (sans Internet) :

  1. Visitez le site (même hors ligne)
  2. La recherche fonctionne normalement
  3. Vous voyez les résultats des données enregistrées

Remarque : Les données hors ligne se mettent à jour automatiquement lorsque vous êtes en ligne. Les surfaces hors ligne indiquent aussi la date de la dernière mise à jour et avertissent si la copie en cache peut être obsolète.

docs.title: Qu'est-ce qui fonctionne hors ligne ?
docs.text:
✅ Fonctionne :

  • Rechercher des services
  • Voir les détails du service
  • Lire les descriptions
  • Voir les numéros de téléphone et adresses

❌ Ne fonctionne pas :

  • Cliquer sur les liens de site Web (nécessite Internet)
  • Obtenir des directions en direct
  • Envoyer des courriels
  • Voir les mises à jour en temps réel

docs.title: Nous ne vous suivons pas
docs.text:
Ce que nous NE collectons PAS :

  • Vos recherches (nous ne voyons jamais ce que vous recherchez)
  • Votre localisation (reste uniquement sur votre appareil)
  • Vos informations personnelles
  • Votre historique de navigation
  • Votre adresse IP (dans la plupart des cas)

Comment fonctionne la recherche :

  • Toutes les recherches se font sur votre appareil
  • Votre recherche ne va jamais sur nos serveurs
  • Personne ne peut voir ce que vous avez recherché

docs.title: Open Source
docs.text:
Cette plateforme est open source. Cela signifie :

  • Tout le monde peut voir le code
  • Les experts peuvent vérifier que nous disons la vérité
  • Vous pouvez vérifier que nous ne vous suivons pas
  • Le code est à : github.com/jerdaw/careconnect

docs.title: Témoins (Cookies)
docs.text:
Nous utilisons des témoins minimaux uniquement pour :

  • Mémoriser votre choix de langue
  • Faire fonctionner le site (témoins techniques)

Nous n'utilisons jamais de témoins pour :

  • Vous suivre sur les sites
  • Vous montrer des publicités
  • Vendre vos données

docs.title: Ce service est-il officiel ?
docs.text:
Non. Ce n'est pas un site web officiel de la Ville de Kingston ou du gouvernement.

Nous sommes un projet communautaire. Les informations sur les services sont :

  • Collectées à partir de sources publiques
  • Vérifiées par des bénévoles
  • Mises à jour régulièrement
  • Fournies telles quelles

Vérifiez toujours les détails importants (comme les heures ou l'éligibilité) en appelant directement le service.

docs.title: Est-ce gratuit ?
docs.text: Oui. Cette plateforme est complètement gratuite. Pas de publicité, pas d'abonnement, pas de frais cachés.

docs.title: Dois-je créer un compte ?
docs.text: Non. Vous pouvez rechercher sans vous inscrire ou donner des informations.

docs.title: Comment signaler des informations incorrectes ?
docs.text:
Si vous trouvez des informations incorrectes :

  1. Envoyez-nous un courriel : feedback@careconnect.ing
  2. Dites-nous :
  3. Quel service a des informations incorrectes
  4. Ce qui est incorrect
  5. Ce que cela devrait dire (si vous le savez)

Nous le corrigerons rapidement.

docs.title: Pourquoi [mon service] n'est-il pas listé ?
docs.text:
Nous nous concentrons sur les services sociaux à fort impact à Kingston. Si un service est manquant :

  1. Envoyez-nous un courriel : feedback@careconnect.ing
  2. Incluez :
  3. Nom du service
  4. Ce qu'ils font
  5. Coordonnées
  6. Site Web (s'ils en ont un)

Nous examinerons et l'ajouterons s'il correspond à nos critères.

docs.title: Puis-je utiliser ceci sur mon téléphone ?
docs.text:
Oui ! Le site fonctionne sur :

  • iPhones et iPads
  • Téléphones et tablettes Android
  • Tout appareil avec un navigateur Web

Il fonctionne également hors ligne (voir Mode hors ligne).

docs.title: Quels navigateurs fonctionnent ?
docs.text:
Navigateurs modernes :

  • ✅ Chrome (recommandé)
  • ✅ Firefox
  • ✅ Safari
  • ✅ Edge

Navigateurs plus anciens :

  • ⚠️ Internet Explorer : Non pris en charge
  • ⚠️ Navigateurs d'avant 2020 : Peuvent ne pas fonctionner complètement

docs.title: Mes données sont-elles en sécurité ?
docs.text:
Oui. Nous ne collectons pas vos données, donc il n'y a rien à divulguer ou à pirater.

Vos recherches et votre localisation ne quittent jamais votre appareil.

docs.title: Vous avez des difficultés ?
docs.text:
Si le site ne fonctionne pas :

  1. Essayez d'actualiser la page (appuyez sur F5 ou cliquez sur recharger)
  2. Vérifiez votre connexion Internet
  3. Essayez un autre navigateur (comme Chrome ou Firefox)
  4. Effacez le cache de votre navigateur

docs.title: Contactez-nous
docs.text:
Pour des questions ou des commentaires :

  • Courriel : feedback@careconnect.ing
  • Problèmes GitHub : github.com/jerdaw/careconnect/issues

Nous répondons généralement dans les 2-3 jours ouvrables.

docs.title: Aide d'urgence
docs.text:
Si vous êtes en crise :

  • Crise de suicide/santé mentale : Appelez ou textez 988
  • Danger immédiat : Appelez le 911
  • Centre de détresse Kingston : 613-544-1771 (24/7)

docs.title: Conseils rapides
docs.text: ✅ Utilisez des mots de recherche simples (« nourriture » pas « aide alimentaire d'urgence ») ✅ Essayez différents mots si vous ne trouvez pas ce dont vous avez besoin ✅ Vérifiez les badges de vérification (N2 et N3 sont les plus fiables) ✅ Appelez les services pour confirmer les heures et l'éligibilité ✅ Utilisez les catégories si la recherche ne fonctionne pas ✅ Activez la localisation pour voir les services les plus proches ✅ Ça fonctionne hors ligne - économisez les données mobiles ✅ Aucun compte nécessaire - complètement anonyme

docs.title: Déclaration d'accessibilité
docs.text:
Nous nous engageons à rendre cette plateforme accessible à tous, y compris les personnes handicapées.

Normes que nous suivons :

  • WCAG 2.1 Niveau AA (Directives pour l'accessibilité du contenu Web)

Si vous trouvez un problème d'accessibilité :

  • Courriel : feedback@careconnect.ing
  • Nous le corrigerons aussi rapidement que possible

Merci d'utiliser CareConnect !

Nous espérons que cet outil vous aide à trouver le soutien dont vous avez besoin.

Ce guide est disponible en plusieurs langues. Utilisez le sélecteur de langue en haut de la page.

docs.title: CareConnect: User Guide
docs.text: Last reviewed: May 1, 2026 Version: 1.0

docs.title: Welcome
docs.text:
CareConnect helps you find social services in Kingston, Ontario. This guide will show you how to use the platform.

Quick Start:

  1. Go to the website
  2. Type what you need (like "food bank" or "mental health")
  3. See a list of services that can help you
  4. Call, email, or visit the service

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Getting Started
  2. How to Search
  3. Understanding Results
  4. Using the Map
  5. Accessibility Features
  6. Language Options
  7. Offline Mode
  8. Privacy & Your Data
  9. Common Questions
  10. Get Help

docs.title: What is CareConnect?
docs.text:
CareConnect is a search engine for social services. It helps you find:

  • Food banks and meal programs
  • Crisis support (mental health, suicide prevention, domestic violence)
  • Housing help (shelters, rental assistance)
  • Health services (clinics, counseling, addiction support)
  • Legal aid and immigration help
  • Employment and education programs
  • Family services (childcare, parenting support)

We have information on 196 verified services in Kingston.

docs.title: Who Can Use This?
docs.text:
Everyone. This service is free and open to the public.

You don't need to:

  • Create an account
  • Give your name
  • Share personal information

docs.title: Who Made This?
docs.text: This platform was created by community members who wanted to make it easier to find help in Kingston. It is not an official government website.

docs.title: Basic Search
docs.text:
Step 1: Click in the search box at the top of the page.

Step 2: Type what you need. Examples:

  • "food"
  • "mental health"
  • "housing"
  • "free lawyer"

Step 3: Press Enter or click the search button.

Step 4: See your results below.

docs.title: Search Tips
docs.text:
Use simple words:

  • ✅ Good: "food bank"
  • ❌ Too specific: "emergency food assistance program"

Try different words if you don't find what you need:

  • "job help" OR "employment"
  • "counseling" OR "therapy" OR "mental health"

Use categories:

  • Click on category buttons below the search box
  • Examples: Food, Crisis, Housing, Health

Filter by hours:

  • Check "Open Now" to see only services that are currently open

Search near you:

  • Allow location access to see services sorted by distance
  • Or type your neighborhood (like "downtown Kingston")

docs.title: Crisis Searches
docs.text:
If you search for crisis words like "suicide" or "abuse", you will see:

  1. A red banner with emergency numbers
  2. Crisis services at the top of results
  3. Immediate help options (phone, text, chat)

If you are in immediate danger, call 911.

docs.title: Service Cards
docs.text: Each search result shows a service card with:

docs.title: 1. Service Name
docs.text: The organization or program name.

docs.title: 2. Description
docs.text: What the service does and who it helps.

docs.title: 3. Contact Information
docs.text:
• 📞 Phone: Tap to call (on mobile)
  • 📧 Email: Tap to send email
  • 🌐 Website: Click to visit their site
  • 📍 Address: Get directions

docs.title: 4. Hours of Operation
docs.text:
When the service is open. Examples:

  • "Monday-Friday, 9am-5pm"
  • "24/7" (always open)
  • "Call for hours"

docs.title: 5. How to Access (Access Script)
docs.text:
Step-by-step instructions:

  • Who is this for? (eligibility)
  • What to bring (ID, documents)
  • How to get help (walk-in, call, referral needed)

docs.title: 6. Verification Badge
docs.text:
Shows how we verified this service:

  • L1 (Basic): We confirmed it exists
  • L2 (Vetted): We contacted them directly
  • L3 (Verified): Official partnership (best)

Higher levels mean more reliable information.

docs.title: 7. Languages
docs.text: Languages the service offers (English, French, etc.).

docs.title: 8. Accessibility
docs.text: Information about wheelchair access, parking, transit.

docs.title: What If There Are No Results?
docs.text:
If you see "No results found," try:

  1. Use different words
  2. "food" instead of "groceries"

  3. "housing" instead of "shelter"

  4. Check your spelling

  5. Browse categories below the search box

  6. Remove filters (like "Open Now")

  7. Search for a broader topic

  8. "health" instead of "diabetes clinic"

docs.title: View Services on a Map
docs.text:
Step 1: Click the "Map View" button (top right of results).

Step 2: See pins showing where each service is located.

Step 3: Click a pin to see that service's details.

Step 4: Click "Load map preview" if you want to view the embedded map.

Step 5: Click "Get Directions" to open Google Maps directly.

Note: The map preview is off by default for privacy. It only loads after you choose to view it.

docs.title: Allow Location Access
docs.text:
To see services near you:

Step 1: Click "Use my location" or allow location when asked.

Step 2: Your browser will ask permission. Click "Allow".

Step 3: Results will now be sorted by distance from you.

Note: Your location is never saved or shared. It only works on your device.

docs.title: Accessibility Features
docs.text: We built this platform to work for everyone.

docs.title: Keyboard Navigation
docs.text:
You can use this site without a mouse:

  • Tab: Move to the next button or link
  • Shift + Tab: Move back
  • Enter: Click a button or link
  • Spacebar: Check a box
  • Escape: Close a popup

docs.title: Screen Readers
docs.text:
This site works with screen readers like:

  • JAWS
  • NVDA
  • VoiceOver (Mac/iPhone)
  • TalkBack (Android)

Screen reader tips:

  • Results are announced as you search
  • Use headings to jump between sections
  • Service cards have clear labels

docs.title: Text Size
docs.text:
Make text bigger or smaller:

  • In your browser: Press Ctrl + (Windows) or Cmd + (Mac)
  • On mobile: Use your device's accessibility settings

docs.title: High Contrast
docs.text:
If you have low vision:

  1. Turn on High Contrast Mode in your operating system
  2. The site will automatically adjust colors

docs.title: Reduced Motion
docs.text:
If animations make you dizzy:

  1. Turn on Reduce Motion in your device settings
  2. The site will show less animation

docs.title: Change Language
docs.text:
Step 1: Look at the top-right corner of the page.

Step 2: Click the language dropdown (shows your current language).

Step 3: Select your language:

  • English
  • Français (French)
  • 中文 (Chinese)
  • العربية (Arabic)
  • Português (Portuguese)
  • Español (Spanish)
  • ਪੰਜਾਬੀ (Punjabi)

Step 4: The entire site will switch to that language.

docs.title: Service Information Languages
docs.text: Some services offer help in multiple languages. You'll see this in the service card under "Languages Offered".

docs.title: What is Offline Mode?
docs.text:
The site works even without internet. This helps if you:

  • Have a slow connection
  • Are in a place with no WiFi
  • Want to save mobile data

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
First visit (with internet):

  1. Visit the site
  2. The site saves service information on your device
  3. You see a message: "Ready to work offline"

Next visits (without internet):

  1. Visit the site (even offline)
  2. Search works normally
  3. You see results from saved data

Note: Offline data updates automatically when you go online. Offline surfaces also show when the saved snapshot was last refreshed and warn you if the cached copy may be outdated.

docs.title: What Works Offline?
docs.text:
✅ Works:

  • Searching for services
  • Viewing service details
  • Reading descriptions
  • Seeing phone numbers and addresses

❌ Doesn't work:

  • Clicking website links (needs internet)
  • Getting live directions
  • Sending emails
  • Seeing real-time updates

docs.title: We Don't Track You
docs.text:
What we DON'T collect:

  • Your searches (we never see what you search for)
  • Your location (stays on your device only)
  • Your personal information
  • Your browsing history
  • Your IP address (in most cases)

How search works:

  • All searches happen on your device
  • Your search never goes to our servers
  • No one can see what you searched for

docs.title: Open Source
docs.text:
This platform is open source. That means:

  • Anyone can see the code
  • Experts can check we're telling the truth
  • You can verify we don't track you
  • Code is at: github.com/jerdaw/careconnect

docs.title: Cookies
docs.text:
We use minimal cookies only for:

  • Remembering your language choice
  • Making the site work (technical cookies)

We never use cookies for:

  • Tracking you across sites
  • Showing you ads
  • Selling your data

docs.title: Is This Service Official?
docs.text:
No. This is not an official City of Kingston or government website.

We are a community project. Service information is:

  • Collected from public sources
  • Verified by volunteers
  • Updated regularly
  • Provided as-is

Always verify important details (like hours or eligibility) by calling the service directly.

docs.title: Is this free?
docs.text: Yes. This platform is completely free to use. No ads, no subscription, no hidden fees.

docs.title: Do I need to create an account?
docs.text: No. You can search without signing up or giving any information.

docs.title: How do I report wrong information?
docs.text:
If you find incorrect information:

  1. Email us: feedback@careconnect.ing
  2. Tell us:
  3. Which service has wrong info
  4. What is wrong
  5. What it should say (if you know)

We'll fix it quickly.

docs.title: Why isn't [my service] listed?
docs.text:
We focus on high-impact social services in Kingston. If a service is missing:

  1. Email us: feedback@careconnect.ing
  2. Include:
  3. Service name
  4. What they do
  5. Contact information
  6. Website (if they have one)

We'll review and add it if it fits our criteria.

docs.title: Can I use this on my phone?
docs.text:
Yes! The site works on:

  • iPhones and iPads
  • Android phones and tablets
  • Any device with a web browser

It also works offline (see Offline Mode).

docs.title: Which browsers work?
docs.text:
Modern browsers:

  • ✅ Chrome (recommended)
  • ✅ Firefox
  • ✅ Safari
  • ✅ Edge

Older browsers:

  • ⚠️ Internet Explorer: Not supported
  • ⚠️ Browsers from before 2020: May not work fully

docs.title: Is my data safe?
docs.text:
Yes. We don't collect your data, so there's nothing to leak or hack.

Your searches and location never leave your device.

docs.title: Having Trouble?
docs.text:
If the site isn't working:

  1. Try refreshing the page (press F5 or click reload)
  2. Check your internet connection
  3. Try a different browser (like Chrome or Firefox)
  4. Clear your browser cache

docs.title: Contact Us
docs.text:
For questions or feedback:

  • Email: feedback@careconnect.ing
  • GitHub Issues: github.com/jerdaw/careconnect/issues

We usually respond within 2-3 business days.

docs.title: Emergency Help
docs.text:
If you are in crisis:

  • Suicide/mental health crisis: Call or text 988
  • Immediate danger: Call 911
  • Distress Centre Kingston: 613-544-1771 (24/7)

docs.title: Quick Tips
docs.text: ✅ Use simple search words ("food" not "emergency food assistance") ✅ Try different words if you don't find what you need ✅ Check verification badges (L2 and L3 are most reliable) ✅ Call services to confirm hours and eligibility ✅ Use categories if search doesn't work ✅ Turn on location to see closest services ✅ It works offline - save mobile data ✅ No account needed - completely anonymous

docs.title: Accessibility Statement
docs.text:
We are committed to making this platform accessible to everyone, including people with disabilities.

Standards we follow:

  • WCAG 2.1 Level AA (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines)

If you find an accessibility issue:

  • Email: feedback@careconnect.ing
  • We will fix it as quickly as possible

Thank you for using CareConnect!

We hope this tool helps you find the support you need.

This guide is available in multiple languages. Use the language selector at the top of the page.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text: The lib/search.ts file had grown to over 480 lines, handling multiple responsibilities including data loading, vector math, geo-math, keyword scoring, and fuzzy matching. This made it difficult to maintain, test in isolation, and caused linting/complexity issues.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
We decided to split lib/search.ts into a modular directory structure under lib/search/:

  • data.ts: Data loading & caching
  • scoring.ts: Keyword scoring logic
  • vector.ts: Vector/Semantic math
  • geo.ts: Geolocation math
  • fuzzy.ts: Query suggestions
  • utils.ts: Shared text utilities
  • types.ts: Shared interfaces
  • index.ts: Coordination logic (searchServices)

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Positive: Better separation of concerns, easier unit testing of individual components, improved readability.
  • Positive: Easier to extend specific parts (e.g., adding new scoring weights) without touching unrelated logic.
  • Neutral: Slightly more files to manage, but the directory grouping keeps them organized.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text: Next.js applications often suffer from "silent failures" where environment variables (like Supabase URLs or API keys) are missing or misconfigured. Non-null assertions (!) are commonly used but provide poor error messages.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
We implemented strict environment validation using @t3-oss/env-nextjs and zod:

  • Created lib/env.ts to define the schema for both server-side and client-side (public) environment variables.
  • Required NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL and NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY to be present and properly formatted.
  • Updated middleware.ts and other key services to import env from @/lib/env instead of using process.env directly.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Positive: Fail-fast behavior: the app will throw a clear error at startup if variables are missing.
  • Positive: Fully typed environment variables (Integreated into IDE intellisense).
  • Negative: Build will fail if environment variables are not present (unless marked as optional).

docs.title: ADR 003: Librarian Model for Public Search
Status
docs.text: Accepted (Implemented in v13.0)

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
CareConnect currently supports client-side search using a bundled JSON dataset and/or broad Supabase reads. This architecture makes it difficult to:

  • prevent accidental leakage of future internal-only fields (draft services, reviewer notes, partner-only metadata),
  • scale beyond the current dataset without increasing client payload size,
  • maintain a clear, enforceable public/private boundary as the Partner Portal grows.

The roadmap (v13.0) calls for “Privacy-First Data Fetching” via a server-side “Librarian Model”.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
We will implement a Librarian Model for public search:

  • Public search and service detail fetches go through server-controlled API endpoints.
  • Supabase data is exposed to the public app only via an explicit public projection (services_public) rather than direct reads from the internal services table.
  • Search requests carrying user-entered text use POST (JSON body) to avoid query strings and reduce privacy risk.
  • Personalization and geolocation boosting remain client-side by default.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Positive: Creates a strict boundary that enables future internal notes and draft workflows safely.
  • Positive: Reduces default client payload size and improves scalability.
  • Positive: Supports incremental rollout via feature flags and dual-path fallback.
  • Neutral: Public search becomes a network dependency; offline behavior must be explicitly designed and communicated.
  • Risk: Privacy posture changes (search text may transit the server). Mitigations include POST-only, no query logging, and no tracking.

docs.title: 004 hybrid search ranking multipliers
docs.text:
---
status: stable
last_updated: 2026-04-18
owner: jer
tags: [search, ranking, performance]
---

# ADR 004: Hybrid Search Ranking & Multipliers (v16.0)

## Status

Accepted

## Context

CareConnect's server-side search (Librarian API) initially used basic database filters and simple ordering by verification status and freshness. As our data grew and our ranking requirements became more complex (including authority tiers, completeness boosts, and proximity decay), we needed a way to apply these factors consistently on the server.

The core challenge was that Supabase's PostgREST (via `supabase-js`) does not support complex scoring expressions or deep in-query mathematical boosts easily. Pure SQL implementations would be fragile, hard to maintain, and duplicate the TypeScript scoring logic already developed for client-side search.

## Decision

We implemented a **Hybrid Scoring Strategy** for the Search API (`/api/v1/search/services`):

1. **Candidate Retrieval (SQL)**: Fetch the full filtered candidate set from the `services_public` view using basic `ILIKE` filters and category matches for the current small curated dataset.
2. **In-Memory Scoring (TypeScript)**: The API route uses the same shared ranking pipeline as local search to apply:
   - **Authority Multipliers**: 1.25x for government, 1.15x for healthcare, etc.
   - **Freshness Multipliers**: Decaying boost for recently verified data.
   - **Verification Multipliers**: Boost for L3/L2 verified services.
   - **Completeness Boosts**: Additive points for services with phone, address, hours, etc.
   - **Intent Targeting Boosts**: Additive points for exact matches against `synthetic_queries`.
   - **Resource Capacity Boosts**: Additive points for large-scale organizations.
   - **Proximity Decay**: Multiplier based on distance: `1 / (1 + k * distance)`.
3. **Re-sorting & Pagination**: Sort by the final calculated score and apply `limit`/`offset` in memory before returning the response.

## Consequences

- **Positive**: Single source of truth for scoring logic in TypeScript. Code reuse between client and server modules. Rapid iteration on ranking factors without complex SQL migrations.
- **Positive**: Ability to handle non-geometric proximity (exempting virtual services from decay) easily.
- **Negative**: Full in-memory scoring remains acceptable only while the curated dataset stays small; if the dataset grows materially, DB-side ranking/index work will need a follow-on ADR.
- **Privacy**: No change to zero-knowledge guarantees; scoring happens in the ephemeral API execution context and is not logged.

docs.title: ADR-005: Enhanced Type Safety and API Security
Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
The application faced several technical debt issues related to type safety and consistency:

  1. Implicit any types in API handlers and test mocks, leading to fragile code and potential runtime errors.
  2. Inconsistent validation of API payloads (e.g. POST /api/v1/services), relying on manual checks or implicit casting.
  3. Test files were excluded from linting, allowing code quality to degrade in the test suite (unused imports, broken mocks).
  4. Potential SSR issues with localStorage usage.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
We have decided to enforce strict type safety and validation patterns across the application:

  1. Zod for API Validation: All API write endpoints (POST, PUT, PATCH) must use Zod schemas to validate request bodies before processing.
  2. Rationale: Zod provides runtime validation that infers static TypeScript types, ensuring the runtime data matches our compile-time expectations.
  3. Linting in Tests: The tests/ directory is no longer excluded from ESLint.
  4. Rationale: Tests are first-class code. Ensuring they are linted prevents bit-rot (unused variables, imports) and ensures they remain maintainable.
  5. Consequence: We established a baseline of existing lint errors (mostly any in mocks) to be resolved incrementally, but new tests must be lint-free.
  6. Removal of Global Polyfills: We removed the global SSR localStorage polyfill in favor of explicit checks (typeof window !== 'undefined').
  7. Rationale: Global polyfills can mask real SSR issues. Explicit checks are safer and more transparent.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Positive: API changes are safer; invalid payloads are rejected with clear errors. Tests are cleaner and more reliable.
  • Negative: Slight boilerplate increase for defining Zod schemas. Mocks in tests require more precise typing than any.

docs.title: ADR-006: Security Headers and Content Security Policy
docs.text: Date: 2026-01-15 Status: Accepted Context: Security audit identified missing HTTP security headers

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
Implement comprehensive security headers in next.config.ts:

  1. Content-Security-Policy (CSP)
  2. Restricts script, style, image, font, and connection sources
  3. Prevents clickjacking via frame-ancestors 'none'
  4. Allows OneSignal for push notifications

  5. Allows Supabase connections (including WebSocket)

  6. Additional Headers

  7. X-Frame-Options: DENY — Prevents embedding in iframes
  8. X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff — Prevents MIME sniffing
  9. Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin — Controls referrer info
  10. X-DNS-Prefetch-Control: on — Enables DNS prefetching for performance
  11. Permissions-Policy — Disables camera, microphone; restricts geolocation to self

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
• Defense-in-depth against XSS, clickjacking, and data injection
  • Required for security compliance and penetration testing
  • unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval needed for Next.js/React compatibility

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Third-party scripts must be explicitly allowlisted in CSP
  • Inline styles work but external stylesheets must come from allowed sources
  • Future integrations will need CSP updates

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• 2025 Security Audit Report
  • next.config.ts

docs.title: ADR 007: API Authorization & Resource Ownership Defense
Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text: As the project transitioned from a single-partner pilot to a multi-organization platform, the API faced "Broken Object Level Authorization" (BOLA) risks. Authenticated partners could potentially modify or delete services belonging to other organizations by simply knowing the service UUID.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: We implemented a "Defense in Depth" strategy for authorization, combining Database-level Row Level Security (RLS) with Application-level assertion helpers.

docs.title: 1. Database Layer (Supabase RLS)
docs.text:
We enabled RLS on all primary tables (services, feedback, audit_logs, etc.).

  • Public access is strictly limited to published = true services.
  • Partner access is gated by an organization_members check.
  • Admin access is gated by a role = 'admin' check in the user's raw_user_meta_data.

docs.title: 2. Application Layer (Assertion Helpers)
docs.text:
To prevent "leaky abstractions" and provide clear error messages (403 vs 404), we introduced a centralized authorization utility at lib/auth/authorization.ts.

  • assertServiceOwnership: Verifies that the authenticated user belongs to the organization that owns a specific service.
  • assertAdminRole: Verifies that the user has platform-wide administrative privileges.

docs.title: 3. Unified Audit Logging
docs.text: All destructive and sensitive operations (Create, Update, Delete, Export, Reindex) are logged to a unified audit_logs table. This provides a clear trail of who performed which action on which resource.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Security: Significantly reduced the surface area for horizontal and vertical privilege escalation.
  • Complexity: Slightly increased code boilerplate in API routes (requires an extra ownership check before mutations).
  • Performance: Negligible impact due to efficient indexing on the organization_members and audit_logs tables.
  • Maintainability: Centralized logic in authorization.ts makes it easier to update security policies platform-wide.

docs.title: ADR 008: Next.js 15 and Web Worker Testing Patterns
docs.text: Date: 2026-01-19 Status: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
The migration to Next.js 15 introduced significant changes to server-side rendering (SSR), particularly the asynchronous nature of cookies() and headers(). Our initial test suite failed because previous mocking strategies were incompatible with these new APIs.

Additionally, our AI system uses Web Workers (webllm.worker.ts) which are difficult to unit test directly in Vitest/JSDOM environments because Worker APIs are not fully supported or behave differently than in browsers.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: We have adopted specific standardized patterns for testing these architectural components.

docs.title: 1. Next.js 15 SSR Mocks
docs.text:
We standardize on a global mocking strategy for Next.js 15 server interfaces using vi.hoisted and Standard Mock Definitions.

Pattern:

  • Use tests/setup/next-mocks.ts for consistent mock implementations.
  • Mock next/headers to return synchronous-behaving mocks for cookies() and headers().
  • Mock @supabase/ssr to return a standardized "Builder Pattern" mock client that supports both Promise-based and method-chaining usage.
// Example Standard Mock
vi.mock("next/headers", () => ({
  cookies: vi.fn().mockReturnValue({
    /* ... */
  }),
  headers: vi.fn().mockReturnValue(new Map()),
}))

docs.title: 2. Web Worker Testing Strategy
docs.text:
We explicitly do not unit test *.worker.ts files directly. Instead, we extract the core business logic into a standalone, testable class/module.

Pattern:

  • Refactor: Move logic from webllm.worker.ts to webllm-engine.ts.
  • Unit Test: Test webllm-engine.ts with standard Vitest patterns.
  • Integration: The worker file becomes a thin shell that imports the engine and handles message passing (tested via E2E only).

docs.title: 3. Centralized Test Fixtures
docs.text:
To avoid "mock drift" where different tests use slightly different versions of data structures, we use centralized fixtures.

Pattern:

  • Place fixtures in tests/fixtures/.
  • Export strongly-typed constant objects (e.g., mockServiceL3).
  • Export factory functions for variations (e.g., createMockService({ ... })).
  • Do not define ad-hoc mock data in test files unless specific to a unique edge case.

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
• Stability: Tests are robust against Next.js internal changes.
  • Maintainability: Fix mock logic in one place (next-mocks.ts) updates all tests.
  • Testability: Complex AI logic is fully unit-testable without browser execution context.
  • Consistency: All developers use the same valid data structures.

docs.title: Negative
docs.text:
• Boilerplate: Tests require standardized setup code at the top of files.
  • Indirection: Worker logic is split across two files (worker shell + engine), which may be less intuitive for new contributors.

docs.title: Compliance
docs.text: All new tests MUST follow these patterns. Existing tests have been migrated.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
As of 2026-01-21, CareConnect maintained 196 verified services with significant data gaps:

  • 75% missing scope field
  • 91% missing coordinates
  • 73% missing access_script
  • 62% missing structured hours

We needed a standard, cost-effective process for addressing these gaps that leverages existing paid tools (ChatGPT Plus, Google AI Pro) and free-tier APIs.

Note: These metrics change over time. Recompute current gaps with npm run audit:data.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: We adopt a tiered enrichment strategy:

docs.title: 1. Rule-Based Inference (Scope)
docs.text:
Assign geographic scope using deterministic rules:

  • Local addresses → "kingston"
  • Crisis/telehealth → "ontario" or "canada"
  • National services → "canada"

Rationale: 90%+ of scope assignments can be automated; AI review only for edge cases.

docs.title: 2. OpenCage Geocoding (Coordinates)
docs.text:
Use OpenCage API for batch geocoding.

Rationale:

  • Free tier (2,500 calls/day) sufficient for 179 services
  • Better Canadian address accuracy than Nominatim
  • Caching prevents redundant calls

docs.title: 3. Category-Based Templates (Access Scripts)
docs.text:
Generate access scripts using category templates:

  • Crisis: confidentiality, judgment-free, can end anytime
  • Health: booking, fees, documents
  • Housing: eligibility, process

Rationale: Reduces per-service customization; templates cover 80% of content.

docs.title: 4. Automated Readability Scoring (Plain Language)
docs.text:
Use Flesch-Kincaid algorithm locally.

Rationale: No API cost, deterministic, can be run on every build.

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
• Zero marginal cost for scope, hours, and plain language
  • Geocoding within free tier limits
  • Leverages existing ChatGPT/Gemini subscriptions
  • Repeatable process documented in SOP

docs.title: Neutral
docs.text:
• L3 verification still requires human outreach (cannot be automated)
  • OpenCage requires API key management

docs.title: Negative
docs.text:
• 10-20 services may require manual geocoding
  • Complex hours formats may need AI interpretation

docs.title: ADR-010: PWA Offline Fallback, Public Export, and Workbox Caching
docs.text: Date: 2026-01-22 Status: Accepted Context: v17.6 PWA enhancement for installability + offline-first usability across 7 locales.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
1. Stable navigation fallback URL
  2. Use Workbox navigation fallback fallbacks.document = "/offline" in next.config.ts.
  3. Rewrite /offline → /{locale}/offline in middleware.ts using the user’s NEXT_LOCALE (or default locale).

  4. Ensure next-intl message loading uses the route locale (e.g. getMessages({ locale }) in app/[locale]/layout.tsx) so rewritten routes render the correct language.

  5. Public offline export endpoint (sanitized)

  6. Serve offline bootstrap data from GET /api/v1/services/export.
  7. The export response must omit internal/admin-only fields (e.g., admin_notes, review metadata, org IDs).

  8. Embeddings are shipped as a separate array to match IndexedDB storage patterns.

  9. Workbox caching strategy (privacy-preserving)

  10. Cache /_next/static/** aggressively (immutable build artifacts).
  11. Cache install assets (/manifest.json, /icons/**, /screenshots/**) with CacheFirst.
  12. Use NetworkFirst for /api/v1/services/export so data stays fresh when online but remains usable offline.

  13. Avoid caching auth-protected/dashboard responses.

  14. Custom service worker scope

  15. Keep public/custom-sw.js limited to push notification UX (no fetch handlers).

  16. Notification actions should be supplied by the push payload to support multi-lingual titles; avoid hardcoded UI strings in the SW.

  17. Operational health (no tracking)

  18. GET /api/health includes a pwa block verifying presence of the manifest, icons, screenshots, and SW scripts.
  19. This enables monitoring without user identifiers.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
• Multi-lingual offline: /offline must resolve deterministically while still rendering locale-aware content.
  • Governance + privacy: Offline bootstrap is public by nature; it must not leak internal/admin fields.
  • Maintainability: Workbox handles caching; custom SW is kept minimal to prevent conflicts with generated SW logic.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• /api/v1/services/export becomes a public interface and must remain sanitized as the Service type evolves.
  • Locale switching can affect the cached /offline response; the caching strategy and app pre-warming should minimize stale-language scenarios.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• docs/planning/archive/2026-01-17-v17-6-pwa-enhancement.md
  • docs/runbooks/pwa-testing.md
  • next.config.ts
  • middleware.ts
  • app/api/v1/services/export/route.ts

docs.title: ADR 011: AI Deep Research Output Ingestion (Audit-First)
Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
CareConnect uses manual curation over automatic extraction. We sometimes use “Deep Research” LLM workflows (ChatGPT / Gemini) to accelerate enrichment of missing structured fields in data/services.json, especially:

  • hours (structured; used by the “Open Now” filter)
  • access_script (phone-anxiety support; “what to say when you call”)

Deep Research outputs are not authoritative by default, can be inconsistent in format, and may include brittle or missing evidence links. We need an ingestion process that:

  • Preserves auditability (“what the model said”)
  • Avoids accidental overwrites of curated fields (governance-first)
  • Produces deterministic, merge-ready artifacts
  • Supports targeted QA on high-risk services (especially Crisis)

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
We adopt an audit-first ingestion workflow for AI Deep Research outputs:

  1. Immutable raw archive

  2. Store original model outputs under docs/audits/v17-5/ai-results/raw/.

  3. Normalize separately

  4. Extract and normalize model outputs into merge-ready JSON arrays under docs/audits/v17-5/ai-results/normalized/.

  5. Store any model-provided evidence (e.g., research_sources) separately under docs/audits/v17-5/ai-results/reports/.

  6. Validate before merge

  7. Enforce batch ID alignment (no unknown IDs; correct known, validated drifts only).

  8. Normalize schema shape (e.g., hours day keys, time formats).

  9. No-overwrite-by-default merge

  10. Merge via a dedicated tool (scripts/merge-ai-enrichment.ts) that fills missing fields but does not overwrite existing curated values unless explicitly requested.

  11. Always write a pre-merge backup to data/backups/ (gitignored).

  12. Governance QA

  13. Perform deterministic sampling and record a QA log (Crisis-focused).

  14. Evidence links are availability-checked only; factual verification is completed incrementally for high-risk items.

  15. Evidence strategy

  16. Do not embed per-field evidence URLs into data/services.json by default.
  17. Preserve evidence in the ingestion workspace to avoid schema creep and reduce data drift risk.

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
• Strong audit trail and reproducibility (raw vs normalized vs reports).
  • Lower risk of governance regression (no silent overwrites).
  • Enables future re-processing as schema evolves.

docs.title: Negative / Trade-offs
docs.text:
• More files (raw + normalized + reports) to manage in-repo.
  • Evidence may still be missing or non-actionable for some batches; manual verification remains required for high-risk services.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Data enrichment SOP: docs/governance/data-enrichment-sop.md
  • v17.5 worked example (archived plan): docs/planning/archive/2026-01-23-v17-5-ai-output-ingestion.md
  • Ingestion workspace: docs/audits/v17-5/ai-results/README.md
  • Merge tool: scripts/merge-ai-enrichment.ts

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
We use Materialized Views (MVs) in Postgres to aggregate data for complex queries like feedback_aggregations and unmet_needs_summary. MVs are performant but present a security challenge:

  1. No Row Level Security (RLS): MVs do not support RLS policies directly.
  2. API Exposure: Exposing MVs directly to the Supabase Data API allows anon and authenticated roles to query them if permissions are granted.
  3. Security Warnings: The Supabase Database Linter correctly flags exposed MVs and SECURITY DEFINER views as high-severity risks.

We needed a way to expose this aggregated data to the frontend API securely while satisfying security auditors and linting tools.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
We have adopted a "Secure View Wrapper" pattern for all Materialized Views exposed to the API.

  1. Backing Materialized View (mat_*):
  2. The actual data storage remains in a Materialized View for performance.
  3. These are renamed with a mat_ prefix (e.g., mat_feedback_aggregations).

  4. Permissions: All permissions are explicitly REVOKED from anon and authenticated roles. These views are effectively private to the database owner.

  5. Public API View:

  6. A standard Postgres View is created with the original name (e.g., feedback_aggregations).
  7. This View selects all data from the backing mat_* MV.
  8. Security Invoker: The View is configured with security_invoker = true. This is critical. It ensures the View executes with the permissions of the caller (the user), not the creator (the admin).
  9. Permissions: SELECT permission is granted to anon and authenticated roles as needed.

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
• Security Compliance: Satisfies materialized_view_in_api and security_definer_view lint warnings.
  • Access Control: We can now control access at the View level. If RLS is needed in the future, we can add WHERE clauses to the View that filter based on auth.uid(), effectively polyfilling RLS for the MV data.
  • API Stability: The frontend continues to query feedback_aggregations transparently; the implementation detail of the mat_ backing view is hidden.

docs.title: Negative
docs.text:
• Complexity: Requires two database objects for every aggregated resource (MV + View).
  • Maintenance: Schema changes must be applied to both the MV and the View.

docs.title: Automated Verification
docs.text:
We verify this pattern via:

  • Supabase Linter: Checks for security_definer_view and materialized_view_in_api.
  • Schema validation: Ensuring security_invoker property is set.

docs.title: ADR 013: Consolidated RLS Policies and Security Hardening
Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
As the application grew, the Row Level Security (RLS) policies for key tables (specifically services, feedback, and organization_members) became fragmented. This led to:

  1. Overlapping Policies: Multiple permissive policies applied to the same action (e.g., both "Admins can view all" and "Partners can view own"), causing multiple_permissive_policies linter warnings.
  2. Performance Issues: Policies were evaluating auth.uid() and auth.role() repeatedly per row, leading to auth_rls_initplan warnings.
  3. Security Risks: Some policies (e.g., public inserts) relied on overly broad WITH CHECK (true) logic, raising rls_policy_always_true warnings.
  4. Schema Drift: SECURITY DEFINER functions were mutable (lacking fixed search_path), and some tables missed standard updated_at columns.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: We decided to adopt a strict "One Policy Per Action Per Role" (or unified per action) strategy and explicitly harden all database artifacts.

docs.title: 1. Unified RLS Policies
docs.text:
Instead of having separate policies for "Admins" and "Partners" that both allow SELECT, we consolidate them into a single Unified view policy.

  • Logic: (is_admin() OR is_org_member())
  • Benefit: Eliminates policy overlap warnings and makes permission logic easier to trace in a single location.

docs.title: 2. Performance Optimization (InitPlan)
docs.text:
We wrap all stable function calls in subqueries to force Postgres to evaluate them once per query (InitPlan) rather than per row.

  • Before: auth.role() = 'authenticated'
  • After: (SELECT auth.role()) = 'authenticated'

docs.title: 3. Security Hardening
docs.text:
• Search Path: All SECURITY DEFINER functions must explicitly set SET search_path = public, extensions to prevent search path hijacking.
  • Input Validation: Public INSERT policies (e.g., for feedback) must check for required non-null fields rather than checking true.

docs.title: 4. scorched-earth Migration Strategy
docs.text:
To resolve the complex web of legacy policies, we employed a "scorched earth" approach in migration 20260126050000...:

  1. Drop ALL existing policies for a table.
  2. Re-create only the strictly necessary, unified policies. Sth strategy ensures no "zombie" policies remain from previous iterations.

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
• Linter Compliance: The database schema now passes supabase db lint with zero errors or warnings (except intentionally ignored config warnings).
  • Performance: RLS execution is faster due to InitPlan optimization.
  • Maintainability: Fewer, clearer policies are easier to audit.

docs.title: Negative
docs.text:
• Complexity: Unified policies are individually more complex (contain OR logic) than small, granular policies.
  • Migration Risk: The "scorched earth" approach requires careful testing to ensure no valid access paths are accidentally removed.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Supabase Database Linter
  • PostgreSQL Row Security Policies

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
Supabase's performance advisor flagged 33 potential performance issues in the database:

  1. Unindexed Foreign Keys (4 warnings): Foreign key columns without covering indexes, which can impact JOIN performance.
  2. Unused Indexes (29 warnings): Indexes that Postgres has never used according to pg_stat_user_indexes, potentially wasting storage and slowing writes.

Before taking action, a comprehensive codebase analysis was performed to determine:

  • Which foreign keys are actually queried in JOINs or WHERE clauses
  • Which indexes are genuinely unused vs. false positives due to low traffic
  • Whether audit/provenance fields require indexes

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: We adopted a data-driven approach to index management:

docs.title: 1. Audit-Only Foreign Keys: No Indexes
docs.text:
The following foreign keys are write-only audit fields never used in queries:

  • audit_logs.performed_by
  • notification_audit.sent_by
  • organization_invitations.accepted_by
  • organization_invitations.invited_by
  • organization_members.invited_by
  • services.deleted_by
  • services.reviewed_by
  • partner_terms_acceptance.service_id

Decision: Leave these unindexed. Adding indexes would slow down INSERT/UPDATE operations with zero query benefit.

docs.title: 2. Genuinely Unused Indexes: Remove
docs.text:
Through codebase analysis, 18 indexes were confirmed as genuinely unused:

Migration 1 (20260124000001_optimize_indexes.sql):

  • idx_org_invitations_email - Email never filtered
  • idx_org_invitations_token - Token lookup only in DB functions
  • idx_services_scope - Filtering happens client-side
  • idx_services_authority_tier - Scoring done in-memory
  • idx_audit_logs_record - Write-only table
  • idx_audit_logs_performed_by - Write-only table
  • idx_audit_logs_performed_at - Write-only table
  • idx_notification_audit_sent_by - Write-only table
  • idx_notification_audit_sent_at - Write-only table
  • idx_notification_audit_onesignal_id - Write-only table
  • idx_org_members_invited_by - Never queried
  • idx_feedback_status - Client-side filtering only

Migration 2 (20260124000002_remove_unused_indexes.sql):

  • idx_push_subscriptions_categories - GIN index unused (only filters by endpoint)
  • idx_submissions_status - Table never queried
  • idx_submissions_created - Table never queried
  • idx_partner_terms_service_id - Table never queried
  • idx_admin_actions_service - Write-only audit table
  • idx_admin_actions_action_type - Write-only audit table

docs.title: 3. Missing Critical Index: Add
docs.text:
Dashboard queries for service_update_requests were performing table scans:

.eq("requested_by", user.email)
.eq("status", "pending")

Decision: Added composite index idx_service_update_requests_requested_by_status on (requested_by, status).

docs.title: 4. False Positives: Keep
docs.text:
15 indexes flagged as "unused" are actively used but not detected due to:

  • Low query volume (development/staging environment)
  • Queries going through views/functions rather than direct table access
  • Postgres statistics not yet accumulated

These indexes are retained:

  • idx_mat_feedback_agg_service - Used in analytics views
  • idx_analytics_service_id - Used in analytics API
  • idx_analytics_created_at - Time-based analytics
  • idx_feedback_service - Feedback lookups
  • idx_feedback_created - Ordering feedback by date
  • idx_update_requests_service - Dashboard queries
  • idx_update_requests_status - Filter by pending
  • idx_update_requests_requested_by - User-specific queries
  • idx_org_members_org - Heavy usage in member management
  • idx_notifications_read - Unread notification counts
  • idx_org_invitations_org - Invitation lookups
  • idx_org_invitations_expires - Pending invitation cleanup
  • idx_services_deleted_at - Export filtering
  • idx_reindex_progress_status_started - Admin reindex status

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
• Performance: Removed 18 unused indexes, reducing storage overhead and improving write performance
  • Query Optimization: Added 1 critical missing index for dashboard queries
  • Code-First Validation: Every index decision was validated against actual application code, not just static analysis
  • Clear Documentation: Future developers can reference this ADR to understand why certain foreign keys are intentionally unindexed

docs.title: Negative
docs.text:
• Ongoing Warnings: Supabase will continue to flag the 8 unindexed foreign keys and 15 "unused" indexes as INFO-level warnings
  • Manual Validation: Requires periodic review of warnings as the application evolves

docs.title: Neutral
docs.text: • Postgres Statistics: As production traffic increases, Postgres will recognize more of the "unused" indexes as actively used

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
Two migrations were created:

  1. 20260124000001_optimize_indexes.sql: Removed 12 unused indexes, added 1 critical index
  2. 20260124000002_remove_unused_indexes.sql: Removed 6 additional unused indexes

Both migrations use IF EXISTS guards to ensure idempotency.

docs.title: Future Considerations
docs.text:
• Run ANALYZE on tables periodically to help Postgres recognize index usage
  • Re-evaluate warnings after production traffic accumulates for 2-4 weeks
  • Consider removing partner_terms_acceptance, service_submissions, and admin_actions tables entirely if they remain unused

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Supabase Database Linter - Unindexed Foreign Keys
  • Supabase Database Linter - Unused Indexes
  • PostgreSQL Index Usage Statistics

docs.title: ADR 015: Non-Blocking E2E Tests in CI
Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
The CI pipeline has been consistently failing on E2E tests for over 10 consecutive commits on the main branch, while all other quality checks (static analysis, unit tests, build) pass reliably. Analysis of the failures reveals:

  1. Consistent Timeout Failures: E2E tests fail with timeout errors waiting for networkidle state and API responses
  2. Environmental Issues: Failures are infrastructure-related, not code quality issues
  3. Tests wait for /api/v1/services/export endpoint that times out in CI
  4. waitForLoadState("networkidle") consistently exceeds 90s timeout
  5. Blocking Development: Every commit fails CI despite code being correct, creating a "boy who cried wolf" scenario where developers ignore CI failures
  6. Recent History: All 10+ recent commits show the same E2E timeout pattern

Example failures:

  • Accessibility audit timeouts (4 tests)
  • Offline sync test timeouts (waiting for service export API)
  • Interactive navigation timeouts (networkidle state)

The E2E test infrastructure needs investigation and fixing, but this should not block all development work.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Make E2E tests non-blocking in the CI pipeline by adding continue-on-error: true to the test-e2e job.

docs.title: What This Means
docs.text:
Tests still run:

  • E2E tests execute on every main branch push
  • Results are visible in CI logs
  • Playwright reports are uploaded as artifacts

But don't block builds:

  • CI shows ✅ success even if E2E tests fail
  • Developers can merge PRs and push to main without E2E blocking
  • Other quality gates (lint, type-check, unit tests, build) remain blocking

docs.title: Guiding Principle
docs.text:
"CI should help dev work stay on track, not be so cumbersome it halts progress."

CI should catch real code quality issues, not infrastructure problems. Flaky tests that fail for environmental reasons should provide visibility but not block development.

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
• Development Unblocked: Commits with good code quality can proceed without waiting for E2E test fixes
  • Visibility Maintained: E2E test results still available for review in CI logs and artifacts
  • Focus on Real Issues: Developers can focus on actual code problems caught by unit tests and static analysis
  • Separate Investigation: E2E infrastructure issues can be investigated and fixed without pressure

docs.title: Negative
docs.text:
• False Green: CI shows success even if E2E tests are failing
  • Risk of Regressions: E2E regressions won't block deployment (mitigation: manual review of E2E results before releases)
  • Discipline Required: Team must check E2E results manually rather than relying on CI status

docs.title: Neutral
docs.text: • Incentive to Fix: Making tests non-blocking highlights that they need fixing without blocking work

docs.title: Mitigation Strategies
docs.text:
1. Monitor E2E Results: Periodically review Playwright reports even when CI passes
  2. Create Investigation Task: Add E2E test infrastructure investigation to roadmap
  3. Pre-Release Checklist: Require manual E2E test review before production deployments
  4. Re-enable Blocking: Once E2E tests are stable (passing consistently), remove continue-on-error: true

docs.title: 1. Skip E2E Tests Entirely
docs.text: • Rejected: Loses visibility into E2E test health completely

docs.title: 2. Fix E2E Tests First
docs.text: • Rejected: Blocks all development work until infrastructure issues resolved (unknown timeline)

docs.title: 3. Increase Timeouts
docs.text: • Rejected: Masks infrastructure problems; tests already at 90s timeout (excessive for unit tests)

docs.title: 4. Run E2E Only on Demand
docs.text: • Rejected: Loses continuous feedback; better to run and not block than not run at all

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
File Changed:

  • .github/workflows/ci.yml: Added continue-on-error: true to test-e2e job

Configuration:

test-e2e:
  continue-on-error: true # Non-blocking
  timeout-minutes: 30
  # ... rest of config

Other Quality Gates (Remain Blocking):

  • Static analysis (lint, type-check, prettier, security audit)
  • Unit tests (Vitest)
  • Build verification
  • Data validation
  • i18n audit

docs.title: Future Work
docs.text:
1. Investigate E2E Timeout Root Cause:
  2. Why does waitForLoadState("networkidle") timeout in CI but not locally?
  3. Is /api/v1/services/export actually responding in CI environment?

  4. Are Supabase secrets properly configured in CI?

  5. Stabilize E2E Tests:

  6. Replace waitForLoadState("networkidle") with more reliable selectors
  7. Add explicit waits for API responses with better error handling

  8. Reduce test flakiness through better setup/teardown

  9. Re-enable Blocking:

  10. Once E2E tests pass reliably (e.g., 95%+ pass rate over 2 weeks), remove continue-on-error

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• GitHub Actions: Continue on Error
  • Playwright CI History: Last 10+ commits on main all failed E2E tests
  • Discussion: "If CI is failing and we can't get it to pass, should we make the CI checks easier?"

docs.title: ADR 016: Performance Tracking and Circuit Breaker Pattern
Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text: CareConnect relies on Supabase for critical data operations including search, service management, analytics, and authorization. As the platform scales, two key operational concerns have emerged:

docs.title: 1. Performance Visibility Gap
docs.text:
Problem: No systematic way to measure or track performance of critical operations

  • Search latency unknown (keyword, vector, data loading)
  • Database query performance not monitored
  • API response times not tracked
  • No baseline metrics for regression detection
  • Performance degradation goes unnoticed until users complain

Impact:

  • Can't identify performance bottlenecks
  • No data to guide optimization efforts
  • Risk of slow performance degrading user experience
  • Difficult to validate that optimizations actually improve performance

docs.title: 2. Cascading Failure Risk
docs.text:
Problem: No protection against Supabase outages or degraded performance

  • Every request waits for full database timeout (~30s)
  • Failed operations retry indefinitely without backoff
  • Single database failure can cascade across the entire application
  • No graceful degradation when database is unavailable
  • Offline sync hammers failing endpoints

Impact:

  • Poor user experience during outages (long hangs instead of fast fails)
  • Resource exhaustion from repeated failing requests
  • Potential for cascading failures across dependent systems
  • No fallback mechanism when database is unavailable

docs.title: Prior Art
docs.text:
Existing Resilience Mechanisms:

  • ADR-010: PWA offline fallback with IndexedDB caching (client-side only)
  • Search has JSON fallback (but no automatic triggering)
  • Rate limiting on API endpoints (protection from abuse, not internal failures)

Gap: No server-side resilience pattern for database failures

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Implement two complementary operational improvements for v17.5+:

docs.title: 1. Performance Tracking System
docs.text:
A lightweight, opt-in performance instrumentation system:

Core Components:

  • lib/performance/tracker.ts - Wrapper around logger for tracking operations
  • lib/performance/metrics.ts - In-memory aggregation (p50, p95, p99)
  • Environment variable gated: NEXT_PUBLIC_ENABLE_SEARCH_PERF_TRACKING

Instrumented Paths:

  • Search operations (total, data load, keyword scoring, vector scoring)
  • API routes (request-to-response latency)
  • Database queries (by source: Supabase, IndexedDB, JSON fallback)

Design Principles:

  • Non-invasive: <1ms overhead per operation
  • Opt-in: Disabled by default, enabled per environment
  • Development-first: In-memory storage only (no production overhead)
  • Structured: Integrates with existing logger for consistency

docs.title: 2. Circuit Breaker Pattern
docs.text:
A resilience pattern that prevents cascading failures:

Core Components:

  • lib/resilience/circuit-breaker.ts - Generic circuit breaker implementation
  • lib/resilience/supabase-breaker.ts - Supabase-specific wrapper
  • lib/resilience/telemetry.ts - Event logging and monitoring

States:

  1. CLOSED (normal): Requests pass through
  2. OPEN (failing): Requests fast-fail, preventing cascading failures
  3. HALF_OPEN (testing): Allow limited requests to test recovery

Configuration (environment variables):

CIRCUIT_BREAKER_ENABLED=true
CIRCUIT_BREAKER_FAILURE_THRESHOLD=3        # Failures before opening
CIRCUIT_BREAKER_TIMEOUT=30000              # ms before retry (OPEN → HALF_OPEN)

Protected Operations:

  • Database queries (lib/search/data.ts, lib/services.ts, lib/analytics.ts)
  • Service management (claim, update, fetch)
  • Analytics tracking (non-critical, graceful degradation)
  • Offline sync (respects circuit state, skips when open)

Design Principles:

  • Fail-fast: Prevent long waits during outages
  • Automatic recovery: Transitions to HALF_OPEN after timeout
  • Fallback-aware: Integrates with existing JSON fallback in search
  • Observable: Logs state transitions for debugging

docs.title: Phase 1: Core Infrastructure ✅
docs.text:
Performance Tracking:

// lib/performance/tracker.ts
await trackPerformance(
  "search.total",
  async () => {
    // Operation here
  },
  { queryLength: query.length }
)

Circuit Breaker:

// lib/resilience/supabase-breaker.ts
const result = await withCircuitBreaker(
  async () => supabase.from("services").select("*"),
  async () => loadFromJSONFallback() // Optional fallback
)

Environment Validation (Zod):

// lib/env.ts
CIRCUIT_BREAKER_ENABLED: z.string().transform(val => val !== 'false'),
CIRCUIT_BREAKER_FAILURE_THRESHOLD: z.string().pipe(z.number().int().positive()),
NEXT_PUBLIC_ENABLE_SEARCH_PERF_TRACKING: z.string().transform(val => val === 'true'),

docs.title: Phase 2: Integration ✅
docs.text:
Files Modified:

  • lib/search/index.ts - Performance tracking for search orchestration
  • lib/search/data.ts - Circuit breaker + performance tracking for data loading
  • lib/services.ts - Circuit breaker for service management (3 functions)
  • lib/analytics.ts - Circuit breaker for analytics (2 functions, graceful degradation)
  • lib/offline/sync.ts - Respects circuit breaker state
  • app/api/v1/search/services/route.ts - Performance tracking for API
  • app/api/v1/health/route.ts - NEW: Health check endpoint

Files Created:

  • lib/performance/tracker.ts (120 lines)
  • lib/performance/metrics.ts (235 lines)
  • lib/resilience/circuit-breaker.ts (265 lines)
  • lib/resilience/supabase-breaker.ts (115 lines)
  • lib/resilience/telemetry.ts (130 lines)
  • app/api/v1/health/route.ts (130 lines)

Tests:

  • tests/lib/performance/tracker.test.ts (16 tests, 100% pass)
  • tests/lib/resilience/circuit-breaker.test.ts (18 tests, 100% pass)

docs.title: Phase 3: Load Testing ✅
docs.text:
k6 Infrastructure:

  • tests/load/smoke-test.k6.js - Basic connectivity (1 VU, 30s)
  • tests/load/search-api.k6.js - Realistic load (10-50 VUs, ramp-up)
  • tests/load/sustained-load.k6.js - Stability test (20 VUs, 30min)
  • tests/load/spike-test.k6.js - Spike/stress test (0-100 VUs in 10s)

Documentation:

  • docs/testing/load-testing.md - Comprehensive guide with thresholds

npm Scripts:

npm run test:load          # Main search API test
npm run test:load:smoke    # Quick connectivity check
npm run test:load:sustained # Long-running stability
npm run test:load:spike    # Spike/stress test

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
Performance Tracking:

  • ✅ Visibility: Clear metrics on operation latencies (p50, p95, p99)
  • ✅ Regression Detection: Can establish baseline and catch degradation
  • ✅ Optimization Guidance: Data-driven decisions on what to optimize
  • ✅ Minimal Overhead: <1ms per operation, opt-in only
  • ✅ Development-Friendly: Works locally without external services

Circuit Breaker:

  • ✅ Fast Failures: Prevents 30s hangs during outages (fail in <1ms)
  • ✅ Graceful Degradation: Automatic fallback to JSON in search
  • ✅ Automatic Recovery: Self-healing via HALF_OPEN state
  • ✅ Resource Protection: Prevents hammering failing endpoints
  • ✅ Observable: State transitions logged for debugging

Operational:

  • ✅ Health Check: New /api/v1/health endpoint for monitoring
  • ✅ Load Testing: k6 scripts provide baseline metrics
  • ✅ Type Safety: All env vars validated via Zod

docs.title: Negative
docs.text:
Performance Tracking:

  • ⚠️ Memory Usage: In-memory metrics store (mitigated: max 1000 samples/operation, 10min retention, dev-only)
  • ⚠️ Per-Process State: Metrics not shared across serverless instances (acceptable for dev/staging)
  • ⚠️ Manual Analysis: No automatic alerting (future: integrate with Axiom/Sentry)

Circuit Breaker:

  • ⚠️ False Positives: Could open on transient errors (mitigated: 50% failure rate threshold + 3 consecutive failures)
  • ⚠️ Per-Process State: Each serverless instance has independent circuit state (acceptable trade-off)
  • ⚠️ Coordination Gap: No distributed circuit state (future: could use Redis if needed)
  • ⚠️ Auth Implications: Circuit breaker on auth queries could lock out users (mitigated: fail-closed security)

Operational:

  • ⚠️ Configuration Complexity: 3 new environment variables to tune
  • ⚠️ Incomplete Rollout: Only ~40% of Supabase calls protected initially (API routes need coverage)

docs.title: Neutral
docs.text:
• k6 Dependency: Adds load testing tool to dev dependencies (~50MB)
  • Code Complexity: +1000 lines of code, but well-tested and isolated

docs.title: 1. Use Supabase Built-in Resilience
docs.text:
Considered: Rely on Supabase client library's retry/timeout mechanisms

Rejected Because:

  • Supabase retries are for network failures, not database unavailability
  • No configurable circuit breaker in Supabase client
  • No fallback mechanism for read operations
  • Doesn't prevent cascading failures

docs.title: 2. External APM Tool (Axiom, Datadog, New Relic)
docs.text:
Considered: Use external Application Performance Monitoring service

Rejected Because:

  • Adds cost and external dependency
  • Overkill for current scale
  • Can be added later (performance tracking provides foundation)
  • Our current logger already supports structured logging for future integration

Future: Once we have baseline metrics from our system, we can selectively send critical metrics to external APM

docs.title: 3. Redis-Based Circuit Breaker
docs.text:
Considered: Use Redis to share circuit breaker state across instances

Rejected Because:

  • Adds infrastructure dependency (Redis)
  • Overkill for current scale (serverless functions are short-lived)
  • Per-instance circuit breaker is sufficient (fail-fast still works)
  • Can be added later if coordination becomes critical

Trade-off Accepted: Each instance independently opens its circuit, but all instances benefit from fast failures

docs.title: 4. API Gateway Circuit Breaker (Vercel Edge Config)
docs.text:
Considered: Implement circuit breaker at API gateway level using Vercel Edge Config

Rejected Because:

  • Tightly couples to Vercel platform
  • Less flexible for fine-grained control
  • Harder to test locally
  • Our approach works everywhere (local, staging, any platform)

docs.title: 5. Prometheus + Grafana
docs.text:
Considered: Full metrics stack with time-series database

Rejected Because:

  • Significant infrastructure overhead
  • Overkill for current needs
  • Can't run locally easily
  • Our in-memory approach is sufficient for development

Future: Can export metrics to Prometheus if scale demands

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Added to .env.example:

# Performance Tracking (v17.5+) - Development/Staging Only
NEXT_PUBLIC_ENABLE_SEARCH_PERF_TRACKING=false

# Circuit Breaker Configuration (v17.5+)
CIRCUIT_BREAKER_ENABLED=true
CIRCUIT_BREAKER_FAILURE_THRESHOLD=3     # Failures before opening
CIRCUIT_BREAKER_TIMEOUT=30000           # ms before retry (30s)

docs.title: Recommended Settings by Environment
docs.text:
Development:

NEXT_PUBLIC_ENABLE_SEARCH_PERF_TRACKING=true  # Enable metrics
CIRCUIT_BREAKER_ENABLED=true
CIRCUIT_BREAKER_FAILURE_THRESHOLD=3
CIRCUIT_BREAKER_TIMEOUT=30000

Staging:

CIRCUIT_BREAKER_ENABLED=true
CIRCUIT_BREAKER_FAILURE_THRESHOLD=5           # Slightly higher threshold
CIRCUIT_BREAKER_TIMEOUT=60000                 # 1min timeout

Production:

NEXT_PUBLIC_ENABLE_SEARCH_PERF_TRACKING=false # Disable in-memory metrics
CIRCUIT_BREAKER_ENABLED=true
CIRCUIT_BREAKER_FAILURE_THRESHOLD=3
CIRCUIT_BREAKER_TIMEOUT=30000

docs.title: Health Check Endpoint
docs.text:
Endpoint: GET /api/v1/health

Response:

{
  "status": "healthy" | "degraded" | "unhealthy",
  "timestamp": "2026-01-25T...",
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "checks": {
    "database": {
      "status": "up" | "down" | "degraded",
      "latencyMs": 45
    },
    "circuitBreaker": {
      "enabled": true,
      "state": "CLOSED" | "OPEN" | "HALF_OPEN",
      "stats": {
        "failureCount": 0,
        "successCount": 125,
        "failureRate": 0.0
      }
    },
    "performance": {  // Only when NEXT_PUBLIC_ENABLE_SEARCH_PERF_TRACKING=true
      "tracking": true,
      "metrics": { /* p50, p95, p99 for all operations */ }
    }
  }
}

Status Codes:

  • 200: Healthy or degraded
  • 503: Unhealthy (circuit open or database down)

docs.title: Circuit Breaker
